retrieved_docs.title: Histrionic Personality Disorder -- Introduction -- Clusters of Personality Disorders in DSM-5-TR
retrieved_docs.content: Cluster A: Cluster A encompasses personality disorders with odd or eccentric characteristics. These include paranoid, schizoid, and schizotypal personality disorders. Individuals within this cluster exhibit social withdrawal, mistrust, and difficulties forming close relationships. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizotypal Personality Disorder -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are few investigations of neuroimaging and histopathological findings among Cluster A personality disorders. In a study involving 20 research participants with either schizoid personality disorder or schizotypal personality disorder, structural alterations in the pyramidal pathway were observed compared to controls. Specifically, research participants presented with greater bilateral white matter volume in the superior corona radiata adjacent to motor/premotor regions. The hypothesized conclusion from these findings suggests that greater volume in motor pathways might relate to cognitive disorganization and negative symptoms in the schizophrenia spectrum illnesses. [24] According to magnetic resonance imaging, patients with schizophrenia and schizotypal personality disorder may share similarities in altered gross morphology of the insular cortex, suggesting a vulnerability factor associated with early neurodevelopmental anomalies and increased brain gyrification in diverse cortical regions. [25] [26] According to a study, patients with schizotypal personality disorder also had a significantly reduced cortical thickness in the left fusiform and parahippocampal gyri, right medial superior frontal gyrus, right inferior frontal gyrus, and right medial orbitofrontal cortex compared to healthy controls. [27] [26] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizoid Personality Disorder -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Investigations of neuroimaging and histopathological findings in cluster A personality disorders are limited. One study involving 20 subjects with either schizoid or schizotypal personality disorder found structural alterations in the pyramidal pathway compared to controls. Specifically, subjects exhibited increased bilateral white matter volume in the superior corona radiata adjacent to motor or premotor regions. The hypothesized conclusion derived from these findings suggests that increased volume in motor pathways may be associated with cognitive disorganization and negative symptoms in schizophrenia spectrum illnesses. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizophrenia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Schizophrenia, a serious mental illness, affects 1% of the global population and is marked by hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, grossly disorganized behavior, and negative signs and symptoms such as reduced emotional expression, avolition, and cognitive impairment. Symptoms generally emerge in late adolescence or early adulthood, and the disorder may be more common in men. Causes include genetic, environmental, and neurobiological factors. No single gene is responsible; there is an interplay of multiple genetic factors. Environmental influences include prenatal and obstetrical risks, psychosocial stressors, and cannabis use. Neurobiologically, schizophrenia is associated with neurotransmitter dysfunction in dopamine and glutamate systems, as well as brain structural changes. Individualized comprehensive management plans, including antipsychotic medications for positive symptoms and evidence-based psychosocial interventions, are essential for improving patient quality of life. This activity describes the epidemiology, genetics, clinical presentation, and management of schizophrenia, providing healthcare professionals with the knowledge and tools to improve patient care for this complex and disabling condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Paranoid Personality Disorder -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: PPD has a consistent overlap with symptom presentation with other cluster A personality disorders, such as deficits in social and interpersonal skills observed in schizoid personality disorder and the odd perceptual experiences in schizotypal personality disorder. These symptoms also overlap with other psychiatric diagnoses, including autism spectrum disorder and schizophrenia spectrum disorders. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizoid Personality Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with cluster A personality disorders are more likely to be biological relatives of an individual with schizophrenia compared to control groups, a finding more strongly associated with schizotypal than schizoid personality disorder. [11] Platelet monoamine oxidase levels, which are associated with sociability, tend to be low in schizotypal personality disorder, although results are inconsistent for schizoid personality disorder. [12] Personality disorders or changes are often associated with medical conditions, including those with pathology, that may damage neurons. These conditions include, but are not limited to, head trauma, cerebrovascular diseases, brain tumors, epilepsy, Huntington's disease, multiple sclerosis, endocrinopathies, heavy metal poisoning, neurosyphilis, and AIDS. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizoid Personality Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Negative symptoms of schizophrenia can overlap with those of schizoid personality disorder. Diminished expression, affective flattening, and alogia are frequently observed in both conditions, potentially leading to similar findings during a mental status examination. While asociality and anhedonia are common in both disorders, apathy is typically associated with schizophrenia and less common in schizoid personality disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Delusional Misidentification Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Schizophrenia: Schizophrenia will also present with other positive symptoms such as hallucinations, cognitive symptoms such as disorganized speech and thoughts, and negative symptoms such as social isolation.

retrieved_docs.title: Schizotypal Personality Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of schizotypal personality disorder is broad. The disorder must be differentiated from schizophrenia, and, as noted in the DSM-5-TR , if the patient proceeds to develop a schizophrenia spectrum disorder, the diagnosis of schizotypal personality disorder must be recorded with the qualifier of premorbid. Schizotypal personality disorder belongs to the Cluster A personality disorders, which are all believed to exist in the psychotic spectrum without formally being psychotic disorders. [1] If a patient meets the criteria for more than 1 personality disorder, all disorders can be diagnosed accordingly. According to the DSM-5-TR , schizotypal personality disorder can be distinguished from other psychiatric disorders as described below.

retrieved_docs.title: Schizotypal Personality Disorder -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Schizotypal personality disorder, as defined by the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision (DSM-5-TR) , is a psychiatric disorder characterized by deficits in social and interpersonal skills, highlighted by a reduced ability to form close relationships, occurring in the setting of eccentric behavior and cognitive or perceptual distortions. Although the DSM continues to categorize schizotypal personality disorder as a personality disorder, the World Health Organization reclassified schizotypal personality disorder as a form of schizophrenia in the International Classification of Diseases, 11th Revision (ICD-11) . The estimated prevalence of schizotypal personality disorder is between 1% and 4%; however, these numbers may be skewed by symptom overlap and possible misdiagnoses of other psychiatric disorders with psychotic symptoms. Schizotypal personality disorder can adversely affect multiple aspects of life, underscoring the need for early intervention and interdisciplinary care. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of schizotypal personality disorder, highlighting the role of the multidisciplinary team in providing care for individuals with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Catatonic Schizophrenia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The group lead by Gabor S.Ungvari discusses the psychomotor symptoms that are seen in chronic schizophrenia and underdiagnosis and recognition of the same. Chronic patients may display more stereotypes, automatic mannerisms, and bizarre postures. [29] This carries a worse prognosis in chronic schizophrenia. [30] Gabor S.Ungvari's group has studied catatonic schizophrenia in the four main domains of motor symptoms, including negative/withdrawn phenomena, automatic phenomena, repetitive phenomena, and agitated phenomena. There are associations between sex, younger age, parkinsonism, and negative symptoms of schizophrenia with catatonia. [10] [30] This group focuses on the presence of psychomotor symptoms in both acute and chronic schizophrenia and the recognition of catatonia within this phenomenon.

retrieved_docs.title: Shared Psychotic Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cognitive impairment: It has been noted that the secondaries lack good judgment and intelligence. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizophrenia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Schizophreniform and brief psychotic disorder: Schizophreniform disorder is diagnosed when psychotic symptoms last less than 6 months, whereas brief psychotic disorder spans from 1 day to less than 1 month. Delusional disorder: Distinguished from schizophrenia by the absence of other characteristic schizophrenia symptoms like pronounced hallucinations or disorganized speech. Schizotypal personality disorder: Characterized by subthreshold schizophrenia symptoms in conjunction with persistent personality traits.

retrieved_docs.title: Schizophrenia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Schizophrenia is a disabling psychiatric condition impacting around 1% of people worldwide and ranking among the top 10 global disability causes. [1] Schizophrenia is characterized by positive psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech, and disorganized or catatonic behavior; negative symptoms such as reduced motivation and expressiveness; and cognitive impairments affecting executive function, memory, and mental processing speed. [2] The effect of schizophrenia on daily life varies greatly, with many individuals facing significant disability and incomplete recovery. Even those with more favorable outcomes confront challenges such as social isolation, stigma, and reduced opportunities for forming close relationships. Unemployment rates among people with schizophrenia are notably high. Factors such as poor diet, weight gain, smoking, and concurrent use of substances are prevalent, collectively shortening life expectancy by an estimated 13 to 15 years. [3] [4] The lifetime risk of death by suicide in patients with schizophrenia is 5% to 10%. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizotypal Personality Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Some common chromosome regions are shared by schizotypal personality disorder and schizophrenia. [6] Among possible candidate genes, the dystrobrevin-binding protein 1 (DTNBP1) gene located on chromosome 6q22.3, which codes for a synaptic protein dysbindin 1, has been associated with schizophrenia and paranoid schizotypy. Functional alterations in the catechol- O -methyltransferase gene (COMT) in the form of a single-nucleotide polymorphism on codon 158, which codes for an enzyme involved in the breakdown of dopamine at the synapse, are believed to contribute to negative and cognitive symptoms in schizophrenia and higher schizotypy scores. [6] According to an older study, platelet monoamine oxidase levels are associated with sociability levels, with low levels occurring in schizotypal personality disorder. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Schizotypal Personality Disorder -- Evaluation -- DSM-5-TR Alternative Diagnostic Model for Schizotypal Personality Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: Criterion A: This criterion  denotes a requirement for moderate or greater impairment in personality functioning. Individuals must demonstrate significant challenges in 2 or more of the following 4 areas. Identity: Confused boundaries between self and others; distorted self-concept; emotional expression often not congruent with context or internal experience. Self-direction: Unrealistic or incoherent goals; no clear set of internal standards. Empathy: Pronounced difficulty understanding the impact of own behaviors on others; frequent misinterpretations of others' motivations and behaviors. Intimacy: Marked impairments in developing close relationships associated with mistrust and anxiety. Criterion B: This criterion outlines  6 pathological personality traits, and diagnosis requires 4 or more of these traits.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 956
retrieved_docs.content: Mental symptom is a broad clinical concept that includes diverse manifestations such as personality problems, loss of pleasure (anhedonia), and self‑destructive behavior. These mental symptoms are linked to psychotic conditions; specifically, they have a broader relationship with transient organic psychotic states (حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة). Transient organic psychotic states are a subtype of organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي), which itself is broadly related to schizophrenic delusion (هوس شَبَقِيّ). The overarching category of psychosis (الذهان) therefore encompasses transient organic psychotic states, situating them within the spectrum of organic psychosis and its association with schizophrenic delusional phenomena. This integrated view highlights how specific mental symptoms connect to distinct forms of organic psychosis and their broader psychotic correlates.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: التالي للولادة (باستثناء الذُهان)”) is recognized as a life‑stage specific subtype within the same framework. Depressive disorders are also linked to psychotic and schizophrenia‑spectrum conditions (SMQ). The psychosis and psychotic disorders category includes affective flattening (“عاطفة متقلصة”) and incorporates several primary depressive disorders—“اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة,” “نوبة واحدة,” “نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة,” “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ,” and “التقلقل الوجداني”—highlighting symptom overlap between mood and psychotic spectra. Unspecified childhood psychosis, classified as an active or current state, belongs to “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” underscoring that psychotic manifestations can emerge early in development. Non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) and non‑infectious delirium/encephalopathy (SMQ) extend the network to neurological disorders. Both conditions feature hypermotor and hypermuscular activity, early‑day hypersomnia, and disturbances of arousal regulation,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2288
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders usually diagnosed in infancy are understood to have a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة, which denotes an unspecified childhood psychosis that is presently active.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: hypersomnia. Indifference belongs to the non‑infectious delirium/brain pathology SMQ, which likewise includes attention deficits, poor concentration and early‑day hypersomnia. Repetitive stereotypic movements and emotional numbness are presented as manifestations of localized brain atrophy “ضمور المخ المحدد.” Organic psychosis is linked to schizophrenic mania, reflecting an intersection between organic mental disorders and schizophrenic patterns. Withdrawal and relapse phenomena feature recurrent tachycardia, underscoring that cardiac symptoms can be part of substance‑withdrawal syndromes. Collectively, these interlinked categories and relationships constitute a comprehensive, multilayered classification system that integrates anxiety, mood, psychotic, developmental, neuro‑organic, psychosexual, substance‑use, and gender‑identity disorders, supporting precise diagnosis, nuanced understanding of comorbidity, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

Additionally, organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) is related to schizophrenic‑like manic symptoms (هوس شَبَقِيّ), indicating a connection between organic psychotic processes and manic presentations of schizophrenia. Overall, the knowledge structure connects sexual orientation and related psychosexual disorders with broader residual and unspecified mental‑health categories, integrates personal attributes and gender identity, and links neuro‑biological findings such as specific brain atrophy to behavioral and emotional manifestations, emphasizing the importance of integrated assessment and classification in clinical practice.

Overall, the model emphasizes the overlap between organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) and manic patterns such as schizophrenic mania (هوس شَبَقِيّ), and highlights how attention deficits, concentration weakness, and daytime hypersomnolence serve as central elements of non‑infectious delirium, guiding clinicians toward precise behavioral and functional assessment and appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 2
retrieved_docs.content: “non‑infectious encephalitis” SMQ, connecting sleep‑wake dysregulation to inflammatory brain processes. Apathy belongs to the “non‑infectious delirium/brain disease” SMQ, which additionally includes attention‑deficit, concentration weakness and early daytime sleepiness, linking cognitive‑motor deficits with delirium‑type brain disease. Gender identity is associated with stereotyped repetitive movements; together with emotional blunting, these signs are interpreted as clinical expressions of localized brain atrophy. Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. A confusional state without acute features and an organic hallucination syndrome are members of the “anticholinergic syndrome” SMQ, reflecting anticholinergic toxicity as a cause of acute psychotic‑like states. Schizophrenic mania (“hous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 57
retrieved_docs.content: المخ المحدد) is identified as the underlying condition that manifests as حركات متكررة نمطية (repetitive stereotyped movements) and تبلد المشاعر (emotional blunting). Organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) is linked with schizophrenic mania (هوس شَبَقِيّ), indicating a wider relationship between organic psychotic processes and manic‑psychotic states. Recognizing that حركات متكررة نمطية and تبلد المشاعر can be clinical expressions of ضمور المخ المحدد, and that ذُهان عضوي is associated with هوس شَبَقِيّ, helps clinicians integrate neurological and psychiatric findings for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

In the **schizophrenia and psychotic disorders SMQ**, mood‑related members include the same depressive diagnoses listed above, **اكتئاب أَوبِيّ** (atypical depression), and **التقلقل الوجداني** (mood lability), with **عاطفة متقلصة** (diminished affect) identified as a characteristic feature. retrieved_docs.content: Senility and organic mental disorders is a diagnostic category that classifies schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, and this category is identified in Arabic as ذُهان شيخوخي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 45
retrieved_docs.content: emotional lability, self‑destructive behavior, emotional poverty, indifference, heightened vigilance, alcohol intoxication, mood swings, memory disorder, psychological distress, weak concentration, reduced affect, motor and muscular hyperactivity, excessive daytime sleepiness, loss of pleasure (anhedonia) and loss of ambition. The non‑infectious delirium/brain‑dysfunction syndrome (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)) is defined by weak concentration and excessive daytime sleepiness, while organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) is linked to manic psychosis (هوس شَبَقِيّ), indicating that psychotic‑like delusions or hallucinations may arise in medically based mental disorders. Together, these hierarchical and intersecting categories provide clinicians with an integrated framework for recognizing overlapping symptom clusters, differentiating primary psychiatric conditions from organic mental disorders, and guiding comprehensive assessment and treatment planning.
